entries.title: When Power Is Supposed to Be Temporary
entries.rawOfficialText: In November 2025, the Supreme Court of Bangladesh restored the practice of caretaker governments. In most countries with parliamentary systems, when an election is about to take place, a caretaker government runs things until the election is over. Learn more about caretaker governments—who staffs them, what are their main responsibilities, and what are they supposed to avoid doing?—and then discuss with your team: should caretaker governments be used in more countries and for longer periods of time? Have any caretaker governments refused to leave office when their time was up?

links.label: Caretaker government — Australian Parliamentary Education Office
Bangladesh Supreme Court reinstates caretaker system - AP

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The Australian Parliamentary Education Office link is core content because it explains what a caretaker period actually means in a parliamentary system: the government remains in office, handles day-to-day business, and avoids major decisions unless it consults the opposition. This should shape the student explanation more than a generic definition would.
The Bangladesh reference in the raw content matters because it shows caretaker government not as a dusty civics term, but as a living constitutional dispute. The 2025 restoration of a nonpartisan caretaker system should be framed as a response to concerns about election fairness, while also raising the risk that temporary arrangements can become politically contested.
For students, the key tension is neutrality vs accountability: a caretaker government may reduce mistrust during elections, but it is still a government with real power in a fragile in-between moment.
entries.studentExplanation:
A caretaker government is a government for the in-between. During an election or transfer of power, leaders may remain in office to keep the country running, but they are expected to avoid major new decisions that should belong to the next fully empowered government. The Bangladesh case in the raw text makes the issue concrete: caretaker systems can be used to protect election fairness, but they can also become political battlegrounds about who should control the transition.

Caretaker governments are useful because they show a formal near-end period in politics. Their job is continuity, not transformation: keep services running, avoid major new decisions, and preserve trust until the next legitimate government arrives. Use this to track how endings begin before they officially happen: what is ending, who notices it first, what signs make it feel near, and why people act differently in the near-end zone.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this explains how some political systems manage power before a new government fully arrives. At the section level, it opens The End Is Nearish by showing that endings in politics are rarely clean: power can become temporary before it disappears. At the year-theme level, it connects to transition and thresholds because the country is neither fully under the old moment nor fully in the new one.
entries.takeaway: It is limited power meant to bridge a transition
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether caretaker systems protect election trust or create new political disputes during fragile transitions.
entries.counterargument: Caretaker governments may reduce mistrust, but they can still be partisan, unelected, or hard to hold accountable.

entries.title: Lame Ducks and Fading Power
entries.rawOfficialText: The United States has no caretaker governments (neither does Venezuela), but it does limit its presidents to two four-year terms in office. As their second term begins to run out, they inevitably find themselves losing power and influence. They are said to become “lame ducks” as the country turns its attention to younger, healthier ducks who might win the next election. Discuss with your team: to prevent lame ducks, would it be better to have no limits on how long a person can lead a country or organization? What would you advise someone wanting to hold onto power for as long as possible?
entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The raw content gives the United States as the major example, so the Twenty-Second Amendment should be preserved as a useful added link. It explains the structural cause of many lame-duck moments in U.S. presidential politics: a president cannot be elected more than twice.
The entry is not only about the phrase “lame duck.” It is about the gap between legal authority and practical influence. A leader may still have the office, staff, and title, but allies, rivals, donors, media, and voters may already be oriented toward the next leader.
The debate should not become simply “term limits good or bad.” It should ask what societies gain and lose when endings are visible in advance: predictability, accountability, weakened influence, succession battles, or protection against permanent rule.
entries.studentExplanation:
A lame duck leader is still in office, but everyone knows the end is coming, so influence starts slipping before the official finish line. In the United States, the two-term presidential limit helps create this problem because a second-term president cannot run again. The real issue is a trade-off: term limits can protect a system from permanent leadership, but they can also make a leader weaker before the term is actually over.

A lame duck has office without the same future leverage. The important “when” is before the term actually ends: allies, opponents, voters, and bureaucracies start reacting to the next leader, so power fades socially before it disappears legally. Use this to track how endings begin before they officially happen: what is ending, who notices it first, what signs make it feel near, and why people act differently in the near-end zone.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students see that political endings can begin before the calendar says they have arrived. At the section level, it extends The End Is Nearish from temporary governments to fading authority. At the year-theme level, it connects to endings and prediction because knowing the finish line changes how everyone behaves before reaching it.
entries.takeaway: Formal office and real influence can diverge
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether term limits protect democracy enough to justify end-of-term weakness.
entries.counterargument: Lame-duck weakness can be a price worth paying if term limits prevent leaders from staying in power indefinitely.

entries.title: If No One Knew Who Was Next
entries.rawOfficialText: Queen Elizabeth I’s solution was to delay naming her successor. “I know the inconstancy of the people of England,” she is said to have told the Scottish ambassador, “How they ever mislike the present government and have their eyes fixed upon the person who is next to succeed.” Discuss with your team: would it be better if no one knew who the next leader would be until after the current leader left (or died)? Can you find an example of such a system?
entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The raw Elizabeth quotation matters because it identifies a psychological mechanism: subjects may “mislike” present government and look toward the next ruler before the current reign ends. This makes succession a mental transition before it is a legal one.
Elizabeth I’s delayed succession is a strong case for the section because it shows that uncertainty can be useful to someone in power. Not naming the future can preserve present loyalty, but it can also store up danger, factional competition, and anxiety.
Students should be pushed toward a nuanced debate: clear succession can stabilize the future, while ambiguous succession can preserve current authority. Both choices have risks.
entries.studentExplanation:
Elizabeth I delayed naming a successor because succession is not just a legal detail; it changes where people place their loyalty. If everyone knows who comes next, the future leader can start shaping the present before the old reign is over. But if no one knows, the current leader may preserve authority while creating uncertainty, rivalry, and fear about what happens after the end.

Elizabeth I’s succession strategy shows uncertainty as a political tool. Naming an heir can stabilize a future transition, but it can also create a rival center of loyalty in the present, so not knowing who is next can help a ruler remain the only focus. Use this to track how endings begin before they officially happen: what is ending, who notices it first, what signs make it feel near, and why people act differently in the near-end zone.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this shows that transitions can begin in people’s minds before they happen officially. At the section level, it deepens The End Is Nearish by asking when an ending truly starts: at death, departure, announcement, or rumor. At the year-theme level, it connects to thresholds and prediction because the future can pull power away from the present.
entries.takeaway: Knowing the next leader can stabilize and weaken
examples: The future leader in the present
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether clear succession plans create stability or weaken the current leader before departure.
entries.counterargument: Keeping succession unclear may preserve current authority, but it can also create instability, intrigue, and conflict later.

entries.title: The Clock That Measures Doom
entries.rawOfficialText: Someday there may be nobody left to lead. Consider the Doomsday Clock, which tries to measure how close we are to the end of human civilization. Discuss with your team: how accurate do you think it is, and in what ways, if any, is it a helpful tool?

links.label: Doomsday Clock - Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The Business Insider link should be preserved, but the official Bulletin links should also be added because they are the primary source for the clock. The Doomsday Clock is not a literal scientific countdown. It is a public-warning symbol set by experts to communicate humanity’s vulnerability to global catastrophe.
The 2026 update matters because the clock is current and changing: the Bulletin set it at 85 seconds to midnight, citing nuclear risk, climate change, disruptive technologies such as AI, biosecurity concerns, and declining international cooperation. This gives the entry a stronger contemporary anchor than the older 2016 article alone.
The student explanation should preserve the central ambiguity: the clock is powerful because it dramatizes risk, but potentially misleading because its precision looks more exact than its underlying expert judgment really is.
entries.studentExplanation:
The Doomsday Clock is not a machine that calculates the exact end of civilization. It is a symbolic warning created by the Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists to make global catastrophic risk visible. The current official links matter because they show the clock is updated as a judgment about changing dangers such as nuclear weapons, climate change, AI, biosecurity, and international conflict. Its strength is also its weakness: it gets attention, but it can look more mathematically precise than it really is.

The Doomsday Clock should be explained as symbolic risk communication, not a literal timer. Its power comes from turning many dangers into one simple image, but its weakness is that the image can look more precise than global risk really is. Use this to track how endings begin before they officially happen: what is ending, who notices it first, what signs make it feel near, and why people act differently in the near-end zone.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students separate symbolic measurement from literal prediction. At the section level, it introduces apocalypse as something people try to communicate through public images and countdowns. At the year-theme level, it connects to prediction and thresholds because the clock makes humanity feel almost at a point of no return.
entries.takeaway: They can motivate concern without being forecasts
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether symbolic public warnings help people confront global risk or oversimplify complex threats.
entries.counterargument: The Doomsday Clock can focus attention, but critics may see it as too subjective or too precise-looking for a symbolic judgment.

entries.title: Why People Keep Predicting the End
entries.rawOfficialText:
Midnight was coming. The world was on fire and people were dying of a terrible illness. Surely, the end of the world was near—or so thought Londoners in 1666. It was also near in 1844, 1910, 1988, 2000 and 2012. Doomsday predictions come and go more frequently in the social media age, but people have been predicting (and rescheduling) the end of the world for as long as they could hold up signs on street corners. With your team, explore the following instances of imminent doom and consider: what do they have in common? What leads people to believe them—and what makes a doomsday prediction go viral? The Millerites (1844) | Halley’s Comet Panic (1910)
The Jupiter Effect (1974) | Hon-Ming Chen (1988) | The Y2K bug (2000)
Large Hadron Collider (2008) | Mayan Apocalypse (2012)

links.label: List of predicted apocalyptic dates — Wikipedia
Ten notable apocalypses that didn’t happen — Smithsonian

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The Wellcome article on 1666 is especially important because it does more than list a failed prediction. It shows why apocalyptic thinking felt plausible: plague, religious conflict, the Great Fire, the number 666, pamphlets, mystical movements, and scientific utopian dreams all mixed into a shared crisis imagination.
The Britannica and Smithsonian links are useful for patterns across cases: Millerites, Halley’s Comet panic, Hon-Ming Chen/True Way, Y2K, and the Mayan Apocalypse all show that failed predictions can come from religion, astronomy, calendars, computing, media panic, or charismatic authority.
The classroom focus should be pattern recognition. Doomsday predictions spread when fear, uncertainty, authority, simple dates, media amplification, and a meaningful story of chaos combine. The question is less “why were they wrong?” and more “why did they feel convincing?”
entries.studentExplanation:
Doomsday predictions often appear when people already feel that the world is unstable. The 1666 link shows Londoners connecting plague, fire, religious conflict, and the number 666 into a story of final judgment. Later examples did the same with comets, calendars, computers, or physics experiments. These predictions fail again and again, but they spread because they turn confusing fear into a simple story with a date, a cause, and a dramatic ending.

The supplemental examples show repeated patterns: ominous numbers, comets, calendars, failed prophecies, cult leadership, computer bugs, and scientific misunderstanding can all become end-times narratives. Students should ask why failed predictions often lead to revision, not disappearance. Use this to track how endings begin before they officially happen: what is ending, who notices it first, what signs make it feel near, and why people act differently in the near-end zone.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students compare failed apocalypses as social and emotional patterns. At the section level, it expands The End Is Nearish from official risk warnings to viral belief. At the year-theme level, it connects to prediction and endings because people often want the chaos of the present to point toward a meaningful final threshold.
entries.takeaway: Chaos becomes easier to handle as a story
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating why apocalyptic predictions spread and why failed predictions often return in new forms.
entries.counterargument: Doomsday predictions can seem irrational, but they often grow from real uncertainty, crisis, and the human search for pattern.

entries.title: Renaissance Visions of the End
entries.rawOfficialText:
The four horsemen of the apocalypse, fires raging across more than California: the Book of Revelation has long offered artists a rich source of imagery for the end of the world. As you explore the following works from the European Renaissance, ask yourself: how does each artist distinguish between the destinations of the saved and the doomed—and is their tone one of terror or acceptance?

Albrecht Dürer | The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse (1498)
Sandro Botticelli | The Mystical Nativity (1500)
Michelangelo | The Last Judgment (1536–1541)
Pieter Bruegel the Elder | The Triumph of Death (c. 1562)

links.label: The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse — Met Museum
The Triumph of Death — AF Prado Museum

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The Hyperallergic link should frame apocalypse art as a long visual tradition, not just a set of scary pictures. It helps students understand why Revelation imagery remains reusable: artists can use the end of the world to think about fear, judgment, crisis, and the order beyond disaster.
The artwork links must be treated as central content. Dürer’s Four Horsemen is kinetic and violent; Botticelli’s Mystical Nativity mixes birth with apocalyptic renewal and demons pushed underground; Michelangelo’s Last Judgment separates saved and damned around Christ’s commanding gesture; Bruegel’s Triumph of Death turns the end into social collapse, where every class is vulnerable.
The slideshow should emphasize comparison: who is saved, who is doomed, what tone dominates, and whether the end feels like terror, justice, warning, order, or acceptance.
entries.studentExplanation:
Renaissance apocalypse art does not simply show destruction. It turns the end of the world into a visual system of judgment: some figures rise, some fall, some are saved, and some are overwhelmed. The links matter because each artwork imagines the final threshold differently. Dürer makes the horsemen feel fast and violent; Botticelli connects apocalypse to renewal; Michelangelo builds a vast moral drama; Bruegel makes death spread across everyday society.

The Renaissance works make apocalypse visual through judgment, hierarchy, scripture, fear, and order. Durer, Botticelli, Michelangelo, Bruegel, Cole, and Martin each choose different signs of finality, so students should ask what kind of ending each image wants viewers to fear or accept. Use this to track how endings begin before they officially happen: what is ending, who notices it first, what signs make it feel near, and why people act differently in the near-end zone.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students read apocalypse as visual storytelling about morality, fear, and destiny. At the section level, it gives The End Is Nearish an art-history dimension. At the year-theme level, it connects to endings and thresholds because these works imagine the most extreme crossing: the final separation between one state of being and another.
entries.takeaway: The end becomes a moral threshold
examples: The Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse (1498)

Pieter Bruegel - The Triumph of Death
Apocalypse art as a visual language
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating how religious art turns fear, judgment, symbolism, and moral order into visual arguments.
entries.counterargument: Renaissance apocalypse art is powerful, but it reflects a specific Christian visual tradition rather than every culture's ending imagery.

entries.title: Modern Apocalypses and New Fears
entries.rawOfficialText:
Consider the more recent apocalypses depicted below. How have they changed as the world has moved toward industrial and digital anxiety—and what specific fears are these artists inviting us to explore?

Thomas Cole | The Course of Empire: Destruction (1836)
John Martin | The Great Day of His Wrath (1851–1853)
Otto Dix | World War I prints (1924)
Viktor Schreckengost | Apocalypse ‘42 (1942)
Harry Louis Freund | Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse (1946)
Saby Menyhei | The Last of Us: Season 1 Concept Art (2025)
Kevin Sherwood & James McCawley | “Where Are We Going?” (2013)

links.label: The Course of Empire: Destruction — Google Arts & Culture
The Great Day of His Wrath — Tate
World War I prints — The Guardian
Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse — Nelson-Atkins

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The modern works should not be treated as another duplicate of Renaissance apocalypse. The shift is conceptual: doom becomes tied to empire, industry, war, fascism, nuclear memory, digital/post-apocalyptic media, and survival rather than mainly divine judgment.
Thomas Cole’s Destruction visualizes imperial collapse and civic violence; John Martin makes catastrophe cosmic and overwhelming; Otto Dix brings apocalypse down into the body and machinery of war; Schreckengost’s Apocalypse ’42 turns fascist leaders and Death into a wartime ceramic protest; Freund reuses the Four Horsemen after global war; The Last of Us concept art and “Where Are We Going?” show how contemporary games and media build apocalyptic mood through emptiness, atmosphere, and sound.
The slides should keep the exact links and exact works. For this entry, images are not optional decorations: the works are the raw content.
entries.studentExplanation:
Modern apocalyptic works often move away from purely religious judgment and toward fears that humans create: empire collapsing, industry failing, war destroying bodies, fascism spreading, cities emptying, and digital media imagining survival after breakdown. The links are the core content because each work shows a different fear of its time. Together, they ask whether the end of the world now looks less like divine punishment and more like the result of human systems losing control.

Modern apocalypse shifts the cause of ruin: war, industry, ideology, disease, technology, and environmental collapse become central. Otto Dix, Schreckengost, Last of Us concept art, and zombie-game music show a world where humans often manufacture the disaster they fear. Use this to track how endings begin before they officially happen: what is ending, who notices it first, what signs make it feel near, and why people act differently in the near-end zone.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students compare how apocalypse changes with history. At the section level, it moves The End Is Nearish from sacred endings to modern anxieties. At the year-theme level, it connects to prediction and the future because every era imagines catastrophe through the technologies, politics, and fears it knows best.
entries.takeaway: Doom reflects the fears of each era
examples: The Great Day of His Wrath (1851–1853)
The Last of Us: Season 1 Concept Art (2025)

entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether modern apocalyptic art reflects public fear or actively shapes how societies imagine collapse.
entries.counterargument: Modern apocalyptic works may use new fears, but many still repeat older patterns of punishment, chaos, and destruction.

entries.title: A Soundtrack for the End
entries.rawOfficialText:
The end of the world can have a soundtrack, too. Consider the musical selections below. What were the circumstances around each one’s creation, and what techniques do they use to achieve their messages and moods?

Olivier Messiaen | Quartet for the End of Time: “Abyss of the Birds” (1940)
Black Sabbath | “Electric Funeral” (1970)
REM | “It’s the End of the World as We Know It” (1987)

links.label: It’s the End of the World as We Know It — YouTube

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The music links are raw content because they let students hear different strategies for imagining the end. Messiaen’s “Abyss of the Birds” comes from Quartet for the End of Time, composed in wartime captivity and connected to Revelation; its slowness and space make time feel suspended.
Black Sabbath’s “Electric Funeral” turns apocalypse into heavy, distorted, nuclear dread. R.E.M.’s “It’s the End of the World as We Know It” does the opposite emotionally: its rapid, crowded, almost cheerful overload makes disaster feel like information chaos and ironic survival.
The slideshow should not use album art as decoration only. Each card should make students listen for technique: tempo, texture, repetition, silence, distortion, speed, density, and tone.
entries.studentExplanation:
Apocalypse can be heard as well as seen. Messiaen makes the end feel suspended, spiritual, and outside ordinary time; Black Sabbath makes it heavy, electric, and nuclear; R.E.M. makes it fast, crowded, and almost funny while still anxious. The links are essential because the lesson is in the sound: different rhythms, textures, silences, and vocal styles create different emotional versions of “the end.”

Sound changes the shape of apocalypse. Messiaen’s prisoner-of-war clarinet makes the end spiritual and suspended, Black Sabbath makes nuclear doom heavy and physical, and R.E.M. turns catastrophe into fast, crowded, almost comic overload. Use this to track how endings begin before they officially happen: what is ending, who notices it first, what signs make it feel near, and why people act differently in the near-end zone.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students understand apocalyptic mood through music. At the section level, it broadens The End Is Nearish beyond paintings and prophecies into sound. At the year-theme level, it connects to endings and the future because music can make an ending feel slow, heavy, chaotic, peaceful, or strangely survivable.
entries.takeaway: Sound shapes the emotional meaning of endings
examples: Quartet for the End of Time: “Abyss of the Birds” (1940)
“It’s the End of the World as We Know It” (1987)
R.E.M. - It’s the End of the World as We Know It
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether music can express crisis and endings more powerfully through mood than explanation can through words.
entries.counterargument: Musical meaning is partly subjective, so listeners may need historical context before making strong claims about apocalyptic sound.

items.note: The audio conveys suspension, birdsong, spiritual stillness, and the feeling that ordinary time has stopped.

entries.title: The Point of No Return
entries.rawOfficialText: Chaos, then catastrophe. Researchers have found that one of the signs that an ecosystem is about to collapse is that things become more volatile—for instance, the amount of chlorophyll in a lake rapidly spikes and plunges. Global climate change is causing something similar: extreme weather events like hurricanes and droughts have grown more frequent. Even flights are facing more turbulence. Discuss with your team: what should we do if the climate passes a point of no return? Would it be okay to spend fewer resources on fighting climate change if the battle is already lost?

links.label: Point of no return — The Ecologist

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The Cary Institute link is a major science source because it explains early-warning signals of ecosystem collapse: systems may become more volatile before they break down, such as unstable spikes and plunges in chlorophyll in lakes.
The Nature Climate Change link strengthens the entry by showing that global warming is intensifying extreme day-to-day temperature changes in many low- and mid-latitude regions, with risks to health, ecosystems, agriculture, and resilience. This makes “chaos, then catastrophe” a scientific pattern rather than just a dramatic phrase.
The BBC turbulence link and Ecologist point-of-no-return debate help translate climate instability into lived experience and ethical debate: if damage is partly locked in, action may shift from total prevention to mitigation, adaptation, justice, and reducing further harm.
entries.studentExplanation:
A point of no return is a threshold after which change becomes extremely difficult or impossible to undo. The links are central because they show warning signs, not just metaphors: ecosystems may become more volatile before collapse, climate change can increase unstable extreme temperature swings, and even flight turbulence can become one everyday sign of a changing atmosphere. The hardest question is not only whether a threshold exists, but how people should act if they fear they are approaching it.

The extra climate notes give students concrete warning signs: ecosystems can show instability before collapse, turbulence can worsen as atmospheric patterns change, and tipping points matter because systems may shift before people agree that they have reached the edge. Use this to track how endings begin before they officially happen: what is ending, who notices it first, what signs make it feel near, and why people act differently in the near-end zone.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this connects collapse to evidence and warning signs. At the section level, it turns apocalyptic thinking away from fantasy and toward measurable real-world systems. At the year-theme level, it connects to prediction and thresholds because “nearish” becomes a question of whether action still matters before or after a dangerous crossing.
entries.takeaway: Warning signs matter because action still shapes outcomes
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether societies should keep acting aggressively even when some climate damage may already be locked in.
entries.counterargument: Calling a situation a point of no return too early can become an excuse for surrender when reducing harm is still possible.

entries.title: Profiting from Disaster
entries.rawOfficialText: Some investors are looking for business opportunities around the warming of the Earth. Explore some of those opportunities, then discuss with your team: should people be allowed to profit from rising temperatures? Could you imagine a company (or even a government) intentionally contributing to climate change, or other potential disasters, to benefit its own interests?

links.label: E&E News version of Politico article

entries.deepLinkAnalysis:
The Politico/E&E link should be treated as the core text of the entry. It is not just about “people making money from climate change” in a vague way; it specifically reports investment in solar geoengineering and planet-cooling technologies with uncertain side effects and weak regulation.
The article creates a subtle ethical distinction: investors may not want catastrophe, but they may profit if governments and societies decide warming has become bad enough to justify risky climate intervention. That is different from ordinary climate adaptation, because the business case may grow stronger as prevention fails.
Students should not be pushed to think every disaster-related business is evil. The important debate is incentive design: preparation can reduce harm, while profit models can sometimes normalize, exploit, or deepen the conditions they claim to solve.
entries.studentExplanation:
Profiting from disaster is not as simple as saying all crisis businesses are bad. People need tools, shelters, insurance, adaptation systems, and climate technologies. But the Politico/E&E link raises a sharper problem: if investors can make billions from risky climate interventions such as solar geoengineering, their financial success may depend on climate failure becoming bad enough to create demand. The ethical question is whether preparation reduces harm or quietly creates incentives to accept disaster.

Solar geoengineering and climate-risk investing make the ethical problem sharper. Preparing for harm is not the same as wanting harm, but if a business grows more valuable as disaster worsens, students should ask who controls the tool and who carries the risk. Use this to track how endings begin before they officially happen: what is ending, who notices it first, what signs make it feel near, and why people act differently in the near-end zone.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students distinguish useful preparation from harmful incentives. At the section level, it gives The End Is Nearish a contemporary economic ending: apocalypse becomes a market opportunity. At the year-theme level, it connects to the future and prediction because some systems may begin planning not to prevent collapse, but to profit from living with it.
entries.takeaway: Useful response and harmful incentive can overlap
examples: Solar geoengineering as a business case
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating where preparation for crisis ends and exploitation of crisis begins.
entries.counterargument: Some crisis investment can be ethical if it reduces harm, funds adaptation, or builds resilience rather than rewarding worsening conditions.
